steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Grande Terre emerged 37 million years ago. Sauquet et al. found the crown age of southern beeches on Grande Terre is 16.4 million years, meaning the clade's most recent common ancestor lived long after the island emerged.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The southern beeches found on Grande Terre are members of a clade that likely originated outside the South Pacific.’: The crown age in the South Pacific matches other regions, so origin outside the South Pacific is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The most recent common ancestor of the southern beeches found on Grande Terre lived at least 37 million years ago.’: 16.4 million years is well after 37 million years ago — this directly contradicts the data. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The southern beeches found on Grande Terre are not members of a clade that existed before the island completely emerged.’: A clade whose common ancestor lived 16.4 million years ago cannot have existed 37 million years ago. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘The ancestors of most species found on Grande Terre arrived on the island earlier than did the most recent common ancestor of the island's southern beeches.’: The passage only provides crown-age data for southern beeches, not for most species on Grande Terre, so this comparative claim is unsupported. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
